# 2.7 Resistance Rolls

## **2.6** 

## **Success Rolls Using Adventurer Level** 

For a success roll, the baseline score is usually calculated as _relevant skill level + ability bonus_ . However, in some cases, _adventurer level_ , rather than skill level, is used to calculate the baseline score. 

For actions that _anyone_ would be able to perform if they became proficient in 

adventuring, the baseline score for a success roll is _adventurer level + ability bonus_ . 

Actions that fit into this category are explained in _6.6: Common Adventurer Abilities_ . In addition, the following _2.7: Resistance Rolls_ are also a check that uses 

adventurer level in its baseline score calculation. 

When a character with adventurer level 0 (a character with no adventurer skills) makes these checks, the baseline score is _0_ regardless of their ability scores. 

## **2.7** 

## **Resistance Rolls** 

A success roll made using _adventurer level + life force bonus_ as the baseline score is specifically referred to as a **life force resistance roll** . Similarly, a success roll made using _adventurer level + mental power bonus_ as the baseline score is specifically called a **mental power resistance roll** , and the two are collectively called simply **resistance rolls** . 

The reason for the name change and the distinction between the two in particular, is that they are frequently performed. 

Therefore, these baseline scores are also defined by the terms **life force resistance** and **mental power resistance** , among others. 

Life force resistance rolls are made to avoid or reduce the effects of physical dangers such as poison, while mental power resistance rolls are made to avoid or reduce the effects of magic or curses. 

In particular, all checks against magic, even if it causes physical damage, are made with mental power resistance rolls. Checks against special attacks by monsters that are magical in nature are also made with mental power resistance rolls. 

master asks us to make a life force resistance roll to see if Ducard II is poisoned by the ghoul. The _toxicity score_ of ghoul poison is 10, which is the target score. Our 2D roll is 5, so with 5+5= _10_ , he escapes paralysis, if barely. 

## **Example 2:** 

Since Ducard II has an adventurer level of 2 and a +2 mental power bonus, the baseline score of his mental power resistance roll (mental power resistance power) is _4_ . While touring an underground cave on an adventure, he wanders into a mysterious room where all four walls are painted in extreme colors. 

In fact, there is a magic trap set in this room that will put anyone who enters it into a deep sleep. The richly colored patterns in the room begin to move strangely, and Ducard II is struck by a strong sense of drowsiness. The game master asks us to make a mental power resistance roll against target score 17. Normally, this would never succeed, but our 2D roll is _12_ ! 

It’s an automatic success. He withstands the magic and escapes the room safely. **Filling Out the Character Sheet** 

The two resistance rolls are frequently performed. Therefore, it’s advisable to make a note of the baseline score ( **resistance** ) so that it is readily available. The Character Sheet provides a field for this purpose. Look at the life force and mental power fields under ability scores. To the right of each of these is a field to write down the bonus, and further to the right of that should be a field labeled _resistance_ . Write the baseline scores of each resistance roll here . 

The baseline score (resistance) equals _adventurer level + life force/mental power bonus_ . 

## **Ability Scores**
